original_abstract:
Text detection in natural scene images is a critical prerequisite for many content-based image analysis tasks. In this paper, we propose an accurate and robust method for scene text detection that leverages the strengths of Maximally Stable Extremal Regions (MSERs). We first design a fast and effective pruning algorithm to extract MSERs as character candidates by minimizing regularized variations. These candidates are then grouped into potential text regions using a single-link clustering algorithm. To optimize this process, we introduce a novel self-training distance metric learning algorithm that automatically determines distance weights and clustering thresholds.

To refine the results, we estimate the posterior probabilities of text candidates being non-text using a specialized character classifier. Candidates with high non-text probabilities are eliminated, and the remaining regions are verified by a final text classifier. We evaluated our system on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition database, achieving an f-measure of over 76%—a significant improvement over the previous state-of-the-art performance of 71%. Further experiments on multilingual, street view, multi-orientation, and born-digital databases demonstrate the versatility and effectiveness of the proposed approach.
humanized_abstract:
Text detection in natural scene images is often a necessary step in many content-based image analysis applications. In this paper, we develop an efficient algorithm for detecting scene text accurately and robustly. We utilize the concepts of Maximally Stable Extremal Regions (MSERs) and contribute to the field by providing a simple yet fast technique for the pruning of MSERs as character candidates based on minimizing the regularized variation. Next, we group the candidates into possible text regions using a single-link clustering algorithm and develop a self-training distance metric learning algorithm to determine the optimal distance weights and clustering thresholds.

This working paper uses the posterior probabilities of the text candidates being non-text estimated by a specialized character classifier to rule out high-probability non-text candidates, and finally uses a text classifier to verify the remaining text candidates. Experimental evaluation was performed on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition (RFC) highly-challenging dataset, achieving an f-measure of 76.2% or higher, representing a significant 5% point increase over the current state-of-the-art result of 71.2% from Jeng et al. (ICDAR '11). Additional experiments on other datasets: (i) multilingual text (ICDAR '11); (ii) street view text extracted from Bing maps; (iii) multi-orientation text (ICDAR '09) and (iv) more modern born-digital text have confirmed the efficacy of our approach.
